local_names.romanized_local: Kǔ Guā (shú) / Peria masak / Pākaṟkāy samaittatu / Karela paka hua

aliases: Peria Katak / Peria Pahit (mentah, biji dibuang)

malay: Peria Katak / Peria Pahit (mentah, biji dibuang)

region_of_origin: South and Southeast Asia (native); widely cultivated in tropical Asia, Africa, and the Caribbean. Commonly consumed in Singapore, Malaysia, India, China, and the Philippines.
food_subgroup: Cooked bitter gourd (Momordica charantia), stir-fried or blanched. ⚠️ G6PD DEFICIENCY WARNING: Vicine content may trigger haemolytic episodes in G6PD-deficient individuals (Singapore prevalence ~3–5%).

ontology.foodex2_description: Bitter gourd/melon (Momordica charantia), cooked, without salt
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00BG used as closest parent; no exact cooked-without-salt subcode confirmed in public FoodEx2 browser. Code retained from input record.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept confirmed for cooked bitter gourd at time of processing.

serving.description: 1 cup cooked (~120g), typical Singapore hawker side dish serving
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical Singapore hawker serving of stir-fried or blanched bitter gourd is approximately 120g (about 1 cup), providing roughly 20 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Balsam-pear (bitter gourd), pods, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC #11719); cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service FoodData Central; National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FDC #11719 provides cooked bitter gourd nutrient values. ICMR-NIN 2017 used for cross-validation of Indian cultivar data. Values represent per 100g cooked (stir-fried or blanched) without added salt.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Bitter Melon (Momordica charantia) — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database low-GI non-starchy vegetable category; supported by published literature on charantin and vicine phytochemical effects on postprandial glucose
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature (Cochrane Review 2012; Leatherdale et al., Br J Nutr 1981)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for cooked bitter gourd in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GI ~15 is a well-supported analogous estimate: bitter gourd has very low digestible carbohydrate (~0.9g net per 100g after fibre subtraction), ~93% water content, and anti-glycaemic phytochemicals (charantin, polypeptide-p). Cochrane 2016 review (Ooi et al.) confirms insufficient evidence to use as T2DM treatment but does not contradict the low GI estimate. GL per 120g serving = (15 × 3.7 × 1.2) / 100 = 0.67, rounded to 0.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low intrinsic sodium (5mg/100g) with meaningful potassium content (296mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Bitter gourd cooked without added sauces is an excellent low-sodium, potassium-rich vegetable for hypertensive patients; however, hawker-style preparations with soy sauce or oyster sauce can add 300–600mg sodium per serving and should be avoided or requested without sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~15 (negligible glycaemic load); anti-diabetic phytochemicals charantin and polypeptide-p
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bitter gourd has a GI of approximately 15 and a negligible glycaemic load (~0.7 per 120g serving), making it safe for blood glucose management in T2DM; while charantin and vicine show modest glucose-lowering effects in short-term studies (0.5–1.5 mmol/L fasting glucose reduction), bitter gourd must NOT replace prescribed diabetes medications as evidence remains insufficient and inconsistent per Cochrane 2016.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low calorie density (17 kcal/100g), negligible saturated fat (0g), high dietary fibre (2.8g/100g), and potassium 296mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bitter gourd is a cardioprotective vegetable with zero saturated fat, negligible cholesterol, high fibre supporting LDL reduction, and potassium supporting vascular tone; it fits well within a heart-healthy dietary pattern when prepared without added oils or high-sodium sauces.

g6pd_deficiency.key_factor: Vicine content — a G6PD-relevant oxidative stressor
g6pd_deficiency.guidance: ⚠️ Bitter gourd contains vicine, a pyrimidine glycoside that can trigger haemolytic anaemia in G6PD-deficient individuals (Singapore prevalence ~3–5%); G6PD-deficient patients should avoid bitter gourd or consult their haematologist before consumption.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (40mg/100g) and folate (51mcg DFE/100g) relevant for RA management
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bitter gourd provides 40mg vitamin C per 100g (supporting collagen synthesis in articular cartilage) and 51mcg DFE folate per 100g, which is clinically relevant for patients on methotrexate therapy where folate supplementation mitigates MTX-related folate depletion.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bitter gourd (Momordica charantia), also known as bitter melon, karela, ampalaya, or peria katak, is a tropical vine vegetable widely consumed across Singapore, Malaysia, India, China, and the Philippines. The cooked form (stir-fried or blanched, per 100g without added salt) provides approximately 17 kcal, 1g protein, 0.2g fat, 3.7g carbohydrate, and 2.8g dietary fibre — making it one of the lowest-calorie vegetables available. Its intense bitter flavour comes from momordicin alkaloids and cucurbitacins. In Singapore hawker culture, it is most commonly stir-fried with eggs (苦瓜炒蛋), used in pork rib soup, or stuffed in yong tau foo. Nutritionally, it is notable for its very low glycaemic index (~15), negligible glycaemic load, high vitamin C (40mg/100g), folate (51mcg DFE/100g), and potassium (296mg/100g). Anti-diabetic phytochemicals — charantin, polypeptide-p, and vicine — have been studied for modest blood glucose-lowering effects, though Cochrane 2016 concluded evidence is insufficient to recommend it as a T2DM treatment. CRITICAL SAFETY NOTE: Vicine in bitter gourd can trigger haemolytic anaemia in G6PD-deficient individuals (Singapore prevalence ~3–5%). Saturated fat is confirmed zero (plant food, no animal fat). Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero (plant-source food). Cholesterol is confirmed zero (plant food). Vitamin D is confirmed zero (non-UV-exposed plant food). Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA FDC #11719. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data not reported in USDA FDC #11719 for this preparation. Water content not separately reported in source used for cooked form.
meal_context: hawker stir-fried bitter gourd with eggs (苦瓜炒蛋) Singapore
bitter gourd pork rib soup Singapore
yong tau foo stuffed bitter gourd Singapore

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC #11719 (cooked bitter gourd, exact match) cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN 2017. GI is an analogous estimate well-supported by the food's biochemical profile and published literature. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchors are defensible.
notes: Saturated fat (0.0g) is naturally zero — bitter gourd is a plant food containing no animal fat. Cholesterol (0.0mg) is naturally zero — plant food contains no cholesterol. Vitamin B12 (0.0mcg) is naturally zero — plant-source food with no B12. Vitamin D (0.0mcg) is confirmed zero — non-UV-exposed plant food per USDA FDC #11719. Trans fat (0.0g) is confirmed zero per USDA FDC #11719. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in USDA FDC #11719 for cooked bitter gourd preparation. water_g set to -1: moisture content not separately reported in the primary source used for this cooked preparation (USDA FDC #11719 reports ~93.8% water for raw form; cooked value not confirmed). GI ~15 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for cooked bitter gourd exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. ⚠️ G6PD DEFICIENCY WARNING: Bitter gourd contains vicine, which may trigger haemolytic anaemia in G6PD-deficient individuals. Singapore G6PD prevalence ~3–5%. Hawker-style preparations (stir-fried with soy sauce or oyster sauce) will significantly increase sodium content beyond the 5mg/100g intrinsic value reported here. ICMR-NIN 2017 is an approved secondary source used for cross-validation only; primary nutrient values are from USDA FDC #11719.
